# Pigo ↔ Pico parity

<!-- Generated by cmd/parity. Do not edit by hand. Run `go run ./cmd/parity --pico-dir …/Pico` to regenerate. -->

Mechanically-generated diff of pigo's public surface vs Pico's, across seven
categories. Pico reference: 7228129cade3f812f22904b503c939b04a75c9dd.

Name surfaces only — behavioural divergence is tracked at the bottom of this
file from hand-curated notes in `docs/parity/exemptions.yaml`.

## Plugin events

- Pigo source: `plugin/events.go`
- Pico source: `lib/Pico.php`

**Shared (28):**
- `onPluginsLoaded`
- `onConfigLoaded`
- `onThemeLoading`
- `onRequestUrl`
- `onRequestFile`
- `onContentLoading`
- `on404ContentLoading`
- `on404ContentLoaded`
- `onContentLoaded`
- `onMetaParsing`
- `onMetaParsed`
- `onContentParsing`
- `onContentPrepared`
- `onContentParsed`
- `onPagesLoading`
- `onPagesDiscovered`
- `onPagesLoaded`
- `onPageTreeBuilt`
- `onPageRendering`
- `onPageRendered`
- `onPluginManuallyLoaded`
- `onMetaHeaders`
- `onYamlParserRegistered`
- `onParsedownRegistered`
- `onSinglePageLoading`
- `onSinglePageContent`
- `onSinglePageLoaded`
- `onTwigRegistered`

**Pico-only (0):**
_(none)_

**Pigo-only (2):**
- `onCurrentPageDiscovered` — Fired per-request once the canonical page for the URL is identified. Pico has no equivalent — a plugin would have to re-resolve the current page from $pages plus the request URL. Pigo lifts that into a first-class event.
- `onThemeLoaded` — Fired after theme resolution; pigo splits Pico's single onThemeLoading event into before/after halves so plugins can react to the resolved theme name.


## Config keys

- Pigo source: `config/config.go`
- Pico source: `config/config.yml.template`

**Shared (20):**
- `site_title`
- `base_url`
- `rewrite_url`
- `debug`
- `timezone`
- `locale`
- `theme`
- `themes_url`
- `theme_config`
- `twig_config`
- `date_format`
- `pages_order_by_meta`
- `pages_order_by`
- `pages_order`
- `content_dir`
- `content_ext`
- `content_config`
- `assets_dir`
- `assets_url`
- `plugins_url`

**Pico-only (1):**
- `my_custom_setting` — Illustrative example in config.yml.template, not a real Pico config key. Pico (and pigo) stash any unrecognised key under the catch-all Custom map. Listed here only because the regex extractor can't tell an example from a real key.

**Pigo-only (5):**
- `log_format` — pigo's structured-logging extension. Accepts text|json.
- `log_level` — pigo's structured-logging (slog) extension. Accepts debug|info|warn|error.
- `plugins` — Pigo plugins are compiled into the binary. This key lets a site enable specific registered plugins by name. Pico's equivalent is the plugin-directory drop-in model plus plugin-specific `PluginName.enabled` keys in config.
- `serve_root_static` — When true (default), pigo serves files sitting under --root (favicon.ico, robots.txt, google-verify tokens) as static files when no page matches. Set to false when fronting pigo with nginx/Apache that serves these already. Pico relies on .htaccess to do the same filtering on the webserver side.
- `template_engine` — pigo supports Go html/template as an alternative to Twig (default twig).


## Template variables

- Pigo source: `render/context.go`
- Pico source: `lib/Pico.php`

**Shared (14):**
- `config`
- `base_url`
- `plugins_url`
- `themes_url`
- `assets_url`
- `theme_url`
- `site_title`
- `meta`
- `content`
- `pages`
- `previous_page`
- `current_page`
- `next_page`
- `version`

**Pico-only (0):**
_(none)_

**Pigo-only (2):**
- `page_tree` — Hierarchical view of pages built from the flat list. Pico exposes the tree to plugins via getPageTree() but not as a Twig variable; pigo makes it directly template-accessible so nav partials don't need a plugin.
- `pages_by_id` — Same data as `pages` but as a Go map for O(1) lookups in plugin code. Themes should prefer `pages` (ordered hash) — Pico templates already did `pages[id]` against the id-keyed assoc array.

## Behavioural divergences

These are not mechanically extracted; they're hand-curated notes on areas
where the two implementations behave differently despite having the same
names.

- **Markdown renderer** — Pico uses Parsedown Extra (PHP); pigo uses goldmark (Go). Output is broadly compatible but whitespace, id-slug rules, and a few extended constructs differ. No programmatic workaround — use a theme-level check if byte-identical output matters.
- **Filesystem case sensitivity** — Page IDs are derived from file paths and are case-sensitive. On a case-insensitive filesystem (macOS HFS+, Windows) two pages whose IDs differ only in case map to the same file, which yields surprising 404s. Use lowercase-only paths to stay portable.
- **Symlinked content** — Pigo's content scanner follows symlinks, matching Pico. Broken symlinks are skipped silently rather than producing a 500.
